perturbation.original_text: Consultant will not, during the Term, and for a period of one (1) year thereafter, directly or indirectly: (i) solicit, recruit or promote the solicitation or recruitment of any employee or consultant of the Company for the purpose of encouraging that employee or consultant to leave the Company's employ or sever an agreement for services; or (ii) solicit, participate in or promote the solicitation of any of the Company's clients, customers, or prospective customers with whom Consultant had a Material Contact (hereinafter defined) and/or regarding whom Consultant received Confidential Information, for the purpose of providing products or services ("Competitive Products/Services").
perturbation.changed_text: Consultant will endeavor not to, during the Term, and for a period of one (1) year thereafter, directly or indirectly: (i) solicit, recruit or promote the solicitation or recruitment of any employee or consultant of the Company if it is practical for the purpose of encouraging that employee or consultant to leave the Company's employ or sever an agreement for services; or (ii) solicit, participate in or promote the solicitation of any of the Company's clients, customers, or prospective customers with whom Consultant had a Material Contact (hereinafter defined) and/or regarding whom Consultant received Confidential Information, for the purpose of providing products or services ("Competitive Products/Services").
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will not' with 'will endeavor not to' makes the non-solicitation agreement less strict and more open to interpretation. Introducing 'if it is practical' adds further ambiguity and subjectivity to the clause. This change weakens the enforceability of the non-solicitation agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements. While non-solicitation agreements are sometimes upheld, the modified language weakens the agreement to the point where it might be deemed an unenforceable restraint of trade because "endeavor not to" and "if it is practical" provide loopholes that undermine the purpose of the clause, making it difficult to enforce.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language weakens the non-solicitation agreement to the point where it could be deemed an unenforceable restraint of trade under California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. Replacing 'will not' with 'will endeavor not to' and introducing 'if it is practical' creates ambiguity and loopholes that undermine the purpose of the clause. Snippet 1 states: 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void'. The changes in the contract weaken it to the point of being potentially void.

perturbation.original_text: Consultant agrees to disclose in writing to the Company all inventions, products, designs, drawings, notes, documents, information, documentation, improvements, works of authorship, processes, techniques, know-how, algorithms, technical and business plans, specifications, hardware, circuits, computer languages, computer programs, databases, user interfaces, encoding techniques, and other materials or innovations of any kind that Consultant may make, conceive, develop or reduce to practice, alone or jointly with others, in connection with performing Services or that result from or that are related to such Services, whether or not they are eligible for patent, copyright, mask work, trade secret, trademark or other legal protection (collectively, "Innovations").
perturbation.changed_text: Consultant agrees to disclose in writing to the Company all inventions, products, designs, drawings, notes, documents, information, documentation, improvements, works of authorship, processes, techniques, know-how, algorithms, technical and business plans, specifications, hardware, circuits, computer languages, computer programs, databases, user interfaces, encoding techniques, and other materials or innovations of any kind that Consultant may, within reason, make, conceive, develop or reduce to practice, alone or jointly with others, in connection with directly performing Services or that predictably result from or are loosely related to such Services, whether or not they are eligible for patent, copyright, mask work, trade secret, trademark or other legal protection (collectively, "Innovations").
perturbation.explanation: The additions of 'within reason', 'directly performing', 'predictably result from', and 'loosely related to' introduce significant ambiguity. 'Within reason' is subjective, while 'directly performing' and 'predictably result from' narrow the scope in a vague way, making it difficult to determine which innovations the consultant is actually obligated to disclose. 'Loosely related to' expands the scope vaguely in a way that could be challenged as overly broad and ambiguous.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code Section 2870(a) states that an employment agreement that requires an employee to assign rights to an invention does not apply to an invention that the employee developed entirely on their own time without using the employer's equipment, supplies, facilities, or trade secret information, except for those inventions that either relate at the time of conception or reduction to practice of the invention to the employer’s business, or actual or demonstrably anticipated research or development of the employer. The added ambiguities create confusion and potential conflict with this law because it becomes unclear what inventions are truly related to the company's business and therefore subject to assignment.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, LAB 2870 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Labor Code - LAB DIVISION 3. EMPLOYMENT RELATIONS [2700 - 3122.4] ( Division 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 2. Employer and Employee [2750 - 2930] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 3.5. Inventions Made by an Employee [2870 - 2872] ( Article 3.5 added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 1001. ) 2870. (a) Any provision in an employment agreement which provides that an employee shall assign, or offer to assign, any of his or her rights in an invention to his or her employer shall not apply to an invention that the employee developed entirely on his or her own time without using the employer’s equipment, supplies, facilities, or trade secret information except for those inventions that either: (1) Relate at the time of conception or reduction to practice of the invention to the employer’s business, or actual or demonstrably anticipated research or development of the employer; or (2) Result from any work performed by the employee for the employer. (b) To the extent a provision in an employment agreement purports to require an employee to assign an invention otherwise excluded from being required to be assigned under subdivision (a), the provision is against the public policy of this state and is unenforceable. (Amended by Stats. 1991, Ch. 647, Sec. 5.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken the obligation of the consultant to disclose inventions. The addition of 'within reason', 'directly performing', 'predictably result from', and 'loosely related to' creates uncertainty regarding which inventions are subject to assignment, potentially conflicting with California Labor Code Section 2870(a). As per snippet 1, Section 2870(a) states an agreement to assign rights does not apply to inventions developed on the employee's time without employer resources, except those related to the employer's business. The added ambiguities obscure what inventions are truly related, thus weakening the original contract's alignment with the law, and creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party hereto agrees that in the event that the other party is required to engage an attorney to enforce any of the terms or obligations contained in this Agreement, the non-prevailing party shall pay all reasonable costs and expenses of that attorney or firm, whether or not a complaint or suit is filed with any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party hereto agrees that in the event that the other party may be deemed to have been reasonably required to engage an attorney to possibly enforce some of the material terms or obligations arguably contained in this Agreement, the non-prevailing party might pay some of the potentially reasonable costs and expenses of that attorney or firm, but only if a complaint or suit is definitively filed with a court of assumedly competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: The changes add multiple layers of ambiguity. 'May be deemed to have been reasonably required' introduces subjectivity. 'Possibly enforce some of the material terms or obligations arguably contained' weakens the obligation. 'Might pay some of the potentially reasonable costs and expenses' makes the payment uncertain. 'Assumedly competent jurisdiction' casts doubt on the court's authority.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1021
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1021 generally allows parties to contract for attorney's fees. However, the added ambiguities make the provision so uncertain that it's questionable whether a court would enforce it. The section requires that the parties' agreement to be upheld, however this highly ambiguous clause could be seen as undermining the very intent of the agreement.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021. Except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties; but parties to actions or proceedings are entitled to their costs, as hereinafter provided. (Amended by Stats. 1986, Ch. 377, Sec. 2.)
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces multiple ambiguities that weaken the enforceability of the attorney's fees provision. The original text states the non-prevailing party 'shall pay all reasonable costs and expenses,' whereas the changed text states the non-prevailing party 'might pay some of the potentially reasonable costs and expenses.' This creates a significant ambiguity, making it uncertain whether attorney's fees will be paid. While CCP 1021 allows parties to agree on attorney's fees, the ambiguities in the changed text undermine the agreement's intent, creating a legal contradiction as defined in the prompt.
